crowdsourced_ids_results.rule_raw: alert http $HOME_NET any -> $EXTERNAL_NET any (msg:"ET MALWARE Kimsuky CSPY Downloader Activity"; flow:established,to_server; http.method; content:"GET"; http.uri; content:"dwn.php?van="; fast_pattern; pcre:"/^\d+$/R"; http.header_names; content:"|0d 0a|Accept|0d 0a|Host|0d 0a|Accept-Encoding|0d 0a|Accept-Language|0d 0a|User-Agent|0d 0a|"; startswith; reference:url,www.cybereason.com/blog/back-to-the-future-inside-the-kimsuky-kgh-spyware-suite; classtype:targeted-activity; sid:2031171; rev:1; metadata:affected_product Windows_XP_Vista_7_8_10_Server_32_64_Bit, attack_target Client_Endpoint, created_at 2020_11_03, deployment Perimeter, former_category MALWARE, malware_family KGH_Malware_Suite, signature_severity Major, tag Kimsuky, updated_at 2020_11_03;)

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: The sample is a packed x86 PE binary exhibiting characteristics of a malicious loader or spyware. It is packed using UPX (indicated by UPX0/UPX1 sections) but includes an additional layer of complex, custom decompression and decryption logic in the entry point. Key indicators include: 1) Anti-analysis/Obfuscation: Extensive use of bitwise rotations (rol), XOR operations, and manual import resolution via LoadLibraryA and GetProcAddress to hide its true functionality. 2) Runtime Memory Manipulation: Uses VirtualProtect to modify page permissions, likely to execute decrypted code in memory. 3) Suspicious Information Gathering: Imports GetUserNameA and SHGetSpecialFolderPathA, which are common for environmental profiling or establishing persistence in specific user directories. 4) String IOCs: Contains a highly suspicious internal build path 'D:\SPY\CSN\On', suggesting a link to spyware families. The combination of packing, manual API resolving, and profiling APIs strongly indicates malicious intent.
